preamble.summary: Jeremiah 21 records the moment King Zedekiah sends a delegation to Jeremiah during the Babylonian siege of Jerusalem, desperately hoping the LORD will intervene as he did in the days of Hezekiah against Assyria. The LORD's answer is devastating: he himself will fight against Jerusalem with outstretched hand and mighty arm — the very language once used of the Exodus deliverance — and will strike down man and beast with pestilence. The chapter closes with a stark choice: those who surrender to Babylon will live, those who remain in the city will die by sword, famine, and plague.
preamble.remarkable: The inversion of Exodus language is the theological earthquake of this chapter. The phrase 'outstretched hand and mighty arm' (v.5) is drawn directly from Deuteronomy 4:34 and 26:8, where it describes God's deliverance of Israel from Egypt. Here the same divine power is turned against his own people. Zedekiah invokes the memory of God's miraculous defense (2 Kings 19:35), but Jeremiah announces that this time God stands with Babylon, not with Jerusalem. The phrase 'I myself will fight against you' (v.5) is covenant-lawsuit language — the divine warrior has switched sides. The choice offered in verses 8-10 — 'the way of life and the way of death' — echoes Deuteronomy 30:15-19, but the 'way of life' is now surrender to the enemy, a scandalous reversal that would have been unthinkable to Zedekiah's court.
preamble.friction: We rendered nilvam ('fight') in verse 5 with the full phrase 'I myself will fight against you' to capture the emphatic first-person construction in Hebrew. The phrase biyad netuya uvizroa' chazaqah ('with outstretched hand and with mighty arm') we preserved in its traditional Exodus form precisely because the inversion depends on the reader recognizing the allusion. The word qetseph ('wrath') in verse 5 we rendered as 'fierce wrath' to distinguish it from other anger vocabulary (af, chemah) that appears in the same passage. In verse 9, 'his life shall be to him as plunder' renders the Hebrew construction wehayetah lo nafsho leshalal — the survivor keeps only his bare life, nothing more.
preamble.connections: The Exodus-inversion language connects to Deuteronomy 4:34 and 26:8. Zedekiah's appeal recalls Hezekiah's deliverance in 2 Kings 19:35 and Isaiah 37:36. The 'way of life and way of death' formula echoes Deuteronomy 30:15-19. The promise that God will fight against his own people fulfills the covenant curses of Leviticus 26:17 and 33. Nebuchadnezzar as God's instrument connects forward to Jeremiah 25:9 where he is called 'my servant.' The siege context continues through Jeremiah 32, 34, and 37-39.

verses.text_hebrew: הַדָּבָ֛ר אֲשֶׁר־הָיָ֥ה אֶֽל־יִרְמְיָ֖הוּ מֵאֵ֣ת יְהוָ֑ה בִּשְׁלֹ֣חַ אֵ֠לָיו הַמֶּ֨לֶךְ צִדְקִיָּ֜הוּ אֶת־פַּשְׁח֣וּר בֶּן־מַלְכִּיָּ֗ה וְאֶת־צְפַנְיָ֤ה בֶן־מַעֲשֵׂיָה֙ הַכֹּהֵ֣ן לֵאמֹֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: The word which came unto Jeremiah from the LORD, when king Zedekiah sent unto him Pashur the son of Malchiah, and Zephaniah the son of Maaseiah the priest, saying,
verses.rendering: The word that came to Jeremiah from the LORD when King Zedekiah sent to him Pashhur son of Malchiah and Zephaniah son of Maaseiah the priest, saying,
translator_notes: This Pashhur is distinct from the Pashhur son of Immer in chapter 20. Zedekiah sends two emissaries — one royal official and one priest — reflecting the gravity of the crisis. The delegation occurs during the Babylonian siege of 588-586 BCE.

verses.text_hebrew: דְּרָשׁ־נָ֤א בַעֲדֵ֙נוּ֙ אֶת־יְהוָ֔ה כִּ֗י נְבוּכַדְרֶאצַּ֛ר מֶ֥לֶךְ בָּבֶ֖ל נִלְחָ֣ם עָלֵ֑ינוּ אוּלַי֩ יַעֲשֶׂ֨ה יְהוָ֤ה אוֹתָ֙נוּ֙ כְּכָל־נִפְלְאֹתָ֔יו וְיַעֲלֵ֖הוּ מֵעָלֵֽינוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Enquire, I pray thee, of the LORD for us; for Nebuchadrezzar king of Babylon maketh war against us; if so be that the LORD will deal with us according to all his wondrous works, that he may go up from us.
verses.rendering: Inquire of the LORD on our behalf, for Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon is making war against us. Perhaps the LORD will deal with us according to all his wondrous deeds, and he will withdraw from us.
verses.expanded_rendering: Nifle'otav ('his wondrous deeds') is the standard term for God's miraculous interventions in Israel's history — particularly the Exodus plagues and the parting of the sea. Zedekiah is hoping for a repeat of Hezekiah's deliverance from Sennacherib (2 Kings 19:35), where the angel of the LORD struck down 185,000 Assyrians overnight.
translator_notes: Zedekiah's 'perhaps' (ulai) reveals both desperation and uncertainty. He does not ask whether God will act but hopes for a miracle on the basis of precedent. The verb ya'aleh ('go up, withdraw') is military terminology for lifting a siege.

key_terms.note: Technical vocabulary for God's acts of power in salvation history, especially the Exodus events. The root p-l-' means to be extraordinary, beyond human capacity.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֥אמֶר יִרְמְיָ֖הוּ אֲלֵיהֶ֑ם כֹּ֥ה תֹאמְר֖וּן אֶל־צִדְקִיָּֽהוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Then said Jeremiah unto them, Thus shall ye say to Zedekiah;
verses.rendering: Then Jeremiah said to them, "Thus you shall say to Zedekiah:
translator_notes: Jeremiah does not soften or delay — his response is immediate and directed back to the king through the same delegation.

verses.text_hebrew: כֹּה־אָמַ֨ר יְהוָ֜ה אֱלֹהֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֗ל הִנְנִ֣י מֵסֵב֮ אֶת־כְּלֵ֣י הַמִּלְחָמָה֮ אֲשֶׁ֣ר בְּיֶדְכֶם֒ אֲשֶׁ֨ר אַתֶּ֜ם נִלְחָמִ֣ים בָּ֗ם אֶת־מֶ֤לֶךְ בָּבֶל֙ וְאֶת־הַכַּשְׂדִּ֔ים הַצָּרִ֣ים עֲלֵיכֶ֔ם מִח֖וּץ לַחוֹמָ֑ה וְאָסַפְתִּ֣י אוֹתָ֔ם אֶל־תּ֖וֹךְ הָעִ֥יר הַזֹּֽאת׃
verses.text_kjv: Thus saith the LORD God of Israel; Behold, I will turn back the weapons of war that are in your hands, wherewith ye fight against the king of Babylon, and against the Chaldeans, which besiege you without the walls, and I will assemble them into the midst of this city.
verses.rendering: This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: I am about to turn back the weapons of war that are in your hands, with which you fight against the king of Babylon and the Chaldeans who besiege you outside the walls, and I will gather them into the midst of this city.
translator_notes: The image is terrifying: God will reverse their weapons — not merely make them ineffective but turn them inward. The Chaldeans besieging from outside will be gathered inside the city walls. The LORD is orchestrating both sides of the battle, and he fights for Babylon.

key_terms.note: The Chaldeans were the ruling dynasty of the Neo-Babylonian Empire. Jeremiah uses 'Chaldeans' and 'Babylon' interchangeably throughout the book.

verses.text_hebrew: וְנִלְחַמְתִּ֤י אֲנִי֙ אִתְּכֶ֔ם בְּיָ֥ד נְטוּיָ֖ה וּבִזְר֣וֹעַ חֲזָקָ֑ה וּבְאַ֥ף וּבְחֵמָ֖ה וּבְקֶ֥צֶף גָּדֽוֹל׃
verses.text_kjv: And I myself will fight against you with an outstretched hand and with a strong arm, even in anger, and in fury, and in great wrath.
verses.rendering: I myself will fight against you with outstretched hand and with mighty arm, in anger, in fury, and in fierce wrath.
verses.expanded_rendering: Beyad netuya uvizroa' chazaqah ('with outstretched hand and with mighty arm') is the signature Exodus phrase describing God's deliverance of Israel from Egypt (Deuteronomy 4:34; 26:8). Here the same divine power is turned against Jerusalem — the God who once fought for Israel at the Red Sea now fights against them. This is the most devastating theological reversal in the chapter.
translator_notes: The emphatic pronoun ani ('I myself') makes God's personal involvement unmistakable. Three anger terms cascade: af ('anger,' literally 'nostril-flaring'), chemah ('fury, heat'), and qetseph ('wrath, indignation'). Each intensifies beyond the last, expressing the fullness of divine judgment.

key_terms.semantic_range: extended hand, stretched-out hand, hand of power
key_terms.note: A fixed phrase in the Deuteronomic tradition for God's sovereign power displayed in the Exodus. Its use here against Israel is a deliberate inversion of the deliverance tradition.

key_terms.semantic_range: strong arm, powerful arm, arm of might
key_terms.note: Paired with 'outstretched hand' as a compound expression for divine power in action. The 'arm' of God is anthropomorphic language for his ability to intervene decisively in history.

verses.text_hebrew: וְהִכֵּיתִ֗י אֶת־יוֹשְׁבֵי֙ הָעִ֣יר הַזֹּ֔את וְאֶת־הָאָדָ֖ם וְאֶת־הַבְּהֵמָ֑ה בְּדֶ֥בֶר גָּד֖וֹל יָמֻֽתוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: And I will smite the inhabitants of this city, both man and beast: they shall die of a great pestilence.
verses.rendering: I will strike the inhabitants of this city, both man and beast. They shall die of a great pestilence.
translator_notes: The language echoes the tenth plague in Egypt (Exodus 12:29) — God strikes inhabitants and animals alike. The pestilence (dever) is one of the covenant-curse triad: sword, famine, and pestilence, which recurs throughout Jeremiah.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאַחֲרֵי־כֵ֣ן נְאֻם־יְהוָ֗ה אֶתֵּ֡ן אֶת־צִדְקִיָּ֣הוּ מֶֽלֶךְ־יְהוּדָ֡ה וְאֶת־עֲבָדָ֣יו וְאֶת־הָעָ֡ם וְאֶת־הַנִּשְׁאָרִ֣ים בָּעִ֣יר הַזֹּאת֩ מִן־הַדֶּ֨בֶר מִן־הַחֶ֜רֶב וּמִן־הָרָעָ֗ב בְּיַ֨ד נְבוּכַדְרֶאצַּ֤ר מֶֽלֶךְ־בָּבֶל֙ וּבְיַ֣ד אֹיְבֵיהֶ֔ם וּבְיַ֖ד מְבַקְשֵׁ֣י נַפְשָׁ֑ם וְהִכָּ֣ם לְפִי־חֶ֗רֶב לֹֽא־יָח֤וּס עֲלֵיהֶם֙ וְלֹ֣א יַחְמֹ֔ל וְלֹ֖א יְרַחֵֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: And afterward, saith the LORD, I will deliver Zedekiah king of Judah, and his servants, and the people, and such as are left in this city from the pestilence, from the sword, and from the famine, into the hand of Nebuchadrezzar king of Babylon, and into the hand of their enemies, and into the hand of those that seek their life: and he shall smite them with the edge of the sword; he shall not spare them, neither have pity, nor have mercy.
verses.rendering: And afterward — declares the LORD — I will give Zedekiah king of Judah, his servants, the people, and those who survive in this city from the pestilence, the sword, and the famine, into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, into the hand of their enemies, and into the hand of those who seek their lives. He will strike them with the edge of the sword. He will not spare them, he will not have pity, and he will not show mercy.
translator_notes: Three negated verbs — 'not spare,' 'not pity,' 'not show mercy' — form a devastating closure. The survivors of pestilence, sword, and famine face yet another fate: captivity and execution. The phrase 'those who seek their lives' (mevaqshei nafsham) is standard Jeremiah vocabulary for mortal enemies.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאֶל־הָעָ֣ם הַזֶּ֔ה תֹּאמַ֕ר כֹּ֖ה אָמַ֣ר יְהוָ֑ה הִנְנִ֤י נֹתֵן֙ לִפְנֵיכֶ֔ם אֶת־דֶּ֥רֶךְ הַחַיִּ֖ים וְאֶת־דֶּ֥רֶךְ הַמָּֽוֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: And unto this people thou shalt say, Thus saith the LORD; Behold, I set before you the way of life, and the way of death.
verses.rendering: And to this people you shall say: This is what the LORD says: I am setting before you the way of life and the way of death.
verses.expanded_rendering: Derek hachayyim vederek hammavet ('the way of life and the way of death') echoes the covenant choice of Deuteronomy 30:15-19 where Moses set before Israel 'life and death, blessing and curse.' But here the content of the choice is scandalously reversed: the 'way of life' is surrender to the Babylonian enemy, a demand that would have seemed treasonous to Jeremiah's audience.
translator_notes: This verse introduces the oracle to the general population, distinct from the message to Zedekiah. The Deuteronomic formula is unmistakable, and its subversion in the following verses would have been profoundly disorienting.

key_terms.semantic_range: path of life, road to living, way of survival
key_terms.note: A Deuteronomic covenantal phrase. In Deuteronomy 30 it means obedience to Torah; here it means surrender to Babylon — a radical redefinition that would have shocked the original audience.

verses.text_hebrew: הַיֹּשֵׁב֙ בָּעִ֣יר הַזֹּ֔את יָמ֕וּת בַּחֶ֖רֶב וּבָרָעָ֣ב וּבַדָּ֑בֶר וְהַיּוֹצֵ֡א וְנָפַ֣ל עַל־הַכַּשְׂדִּים֩ הַצָּרִ֨ים עֲלֵיכֶ֜ם יִֽחְיֶ֗ה וְהָיְתָה־לּ֧וֹ נַפְשׁ֛וֹ לְשָׁלָ֖ל׃
verses.text_kjv: He that abideth in this city shall die by the sword, and by the famine, and by the pestilence: but he that goeth out, and falleth to the Chaldeans that besiege you, he shall live, and his life shall be unto him for a prey.
verses.rendering: Whoever remains in this city shall die by the sword, by famine, and by pestilence. But whoever goes out and surrenders to the Chaldeans who besiege you shall live, and his life shall be to him as plunder.
verses.expanded_rendering: Nafsho leshalal ('his life as plunder') is a striking idiom: the person who surrenders will escape with nothing but his bare life — as if his own survival were war-spoil snatched from destruction. It is life, but life stripped of everything else.
translator_notes: The Hebrew nafal al ('fall upon') means to desert to the enemy. The triad of sword, famine, and pestilence (cherev, ra'av, dever) is Jeremiah's signature judgment formula, appearing over twenty times in the book. The 'life as plunder' idiom also occurs in Jeremiah 38:2, 39:18, and 45:5.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֣י שַׂ֧מְתִּי פָנַ֛י בָּעִ֥יר הַזֹּ֖את לְרָעָ֣ה וְלֹ֣א לְטוֹבָ֑ה נְאֻם־יְהוָ֗ה בְּיַד־מֶ֤לֶךְ בָּבֶל֙ תִּנָּתֵ֔ן וּשְׂרָפָ֖הּ בָּאֵֽשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: For I have set my face against this city for evil, and not for good, saith the LORD: it shall be given into the hand of the king of Babylon, and he shall burn it with fire.
verses.rendering: For I have set my face against this city for harm and not for good — declares the LORD. It shall be given into the hand of the king of Babylon, and he shall burn it with fire.
translator_notes: 'I have set my face' (samti panai) indicates a fixed, irrevocable divine decision. The verb is in the perfect tense — the decision is already made. The burning of Jerusalem is stated as accomplished fact from God's perspective, though it would not occur until 586 BCE.

verses.text_kjv: And touching the house of the king of Judah, say, Hear ye the word of the LORD;
verses.rendering: To the house of the king of Judah: Hear the word of the LORD!
translator_notes: A new oracle begins here, addressed to the royal house as an institution rather than to Zedekiah personally. This introduces the broader theme of royal accountability that continues through chapter 22.

verses.text_hebrew: בֵּ֣ית דָּוִ֗ד כֹּ֚ה אָמַ֣ר יְהוָ֔ה דִּ֤ינוּ לַבֹּ֙קֶר֙ מִשְׁפָּ֔ט וְהַצִּ֥ילוּ גָז֖וּל מִיַּ֣ד עוֹשֵׁ֑ק פֶּן־תֵּצֵ֨א כָאֵ֜שׁ חֲמָתִ֗י וּבָעֲרָה֙ וְאֵ֣ין מְכַבֶּ֔ה מִפְּנֵ֖י רֹ֥עַ מַעַלְלֵיכֶֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: O house of David, thus saith the LORD; Execute judgment in the morning, and deliver him that is spoiled out of the hand of the oppressor, lest my fury go out like fire, and burn that none can quench it, because of the evil of your doings.
verses.rendering: O house of David, this is what the LORD says: Execute justice each morning, and deliver the one who has been robbed from the hand of the oppressor, lest my fury go out like fire and burn with no one to quench it, because of the evil of your deeds.
verses.expanded_rendering: Dinu labboqer mishpat ('execute justice each morning') means the king's first duty every day is to render righteous verdicts — 'morning' is when the court sat at the city gate. Mishpat here is specifically social justice: protecting the powerless from exploitation.
translator_notes: The address to 'the house of David' invokes the dynastic covenant (2 Samuel 7) while simultaneously placing it under judgment. The conditional 'lest' (pen) implies the door is still technically open, though the surrounding context suggests it is nearly shut.

key_terms.semantic_range: justice, judgment, legal verdict, right ruling, ordinance
key_terms.note: One of the most important words in the prophetic vocabulary. Here it refers specifically to the king's obligation to protect the weak through fair legal proceedings.

verses.text_hebrew: הִנְנִ֤י אֵלַ֙יִךְ֙ יֹשֶׁ֣בֶת הָעֵ֔מֶק צ֖וּר הַמִּישֹׁ֑ר נְאֻם־יְהוָ֗ה הָאֹמְרִ֞ים מִ֤י יֵחַת֙ עָלֵ֔ינוּ וּמִ֥י יָב֖וֹא בִּמְעוֹנוֹתֵֽינוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Behold, I am against thee, O inhabitant of the valley, and rock of the plain, saith the LORD; which say, Who shall come down against us? or who shall enter into our habitations?
verses.rendering: I am against you, O inhabitant of the valley, O rock of the plain — declares the LORD — you who say, 'Who will come down against us? Who will enter our strongholds?'
translator_notes: The 'valley' and 'rock of the plain' describe Jerusalem's topography — built on a rocky plateau above the surrounding valleys. The inhabitants' boast reflects false confidence in the city's natural fortifications. The phrase hineni elayik ('I am against you') is the divine war-declaration formula.

verses.text_hebrew: וּפָקַדְתִּ֧י עֲלֵיכֶ֛ם כִּפְרִ֥י מַעַלְלֵיכֶ֖ם נְאֻם־יְהוָ֑ה וְהִצַּ֤תִּי אֵשׁ֙ בְּיַעְרָ֔הּ וְאָכְלָ֖ה כָּל־סְבִיבֶֽיהָ׃
verses.text_kjv: But I will punish you according to the fruit of your doings, saith the LORD: and I will kindle a fire in the forest thereof, and it shall devour all things round about it.
verses.rendering: I will punish you according to the fruit of your deeds — declares the LORD. I will kindle a fire in her forest, and it shall devour everything around her.
translator_notes: The 'forest' (ya'ar) likely refers to the 'House of the Forest of Lebanon' — Solomon's palace complex built with cedar (1 Kings 7:2). The fire that begins in the royal 'forest' will spread to consume everything around it. The phrase 'fruit of your deeds' (peri ma'allelkem) connects judgment to the natural consequences of their own actions.
